member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithAuthority(System.String,System.Boolean)
member.summary:
Important: Use WithTenantId or WithTenantIdFromAuthority instead, or WithB2CAuthority for B2C authorities.
Specific authority for which the token is requested. Passing a different value than configured at the application constructor narrows down the selection to a specific tenant. This does not change the configured value in the application. This is specific to applications managing several accounts (like a mail client with several mailboxes). See https://aka.ms/msal-net-application-configuration.
member.param: Uri for the authority. In the case when the authority URI is a known Azure AD URI, this setting needs to be consistent with what is declared in the application registration portal.
Whether the authority should be validated against the server metadata.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.

member.param: Azure Cloud instance.
GUID of the tenant from which to sign-in users.
Whether the authority should be validated against the server metadata.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithTenantId(System.String)
member.summary:
Overrides the tenant ID specified in the authority at the application level. This operation preserves the authority host (environment).
If an authority was not specified at the application level, the default used is `https://login.microsoftonline.com/common`.
member.param: Tenant ID of the Microsoft Entra ID tenant or a domain associated with this Microsoft Entra ID tenant, in order to sign-in a user of a specific organization only.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.
member.exception: Thrown if is null or an empty string.
Thrown if is not well-formatted (for example, has spaces).
Thrown in more general exception scenarios (for ex. if the application was configured with an authority that does not allow tenants).
member.remarks: The tenant should be more restrictive than the one configured at the application level, e.g. don't use "common". Does not affect authority validation, which is specified at the application level.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithTenantIdFromAuthority(System.Uri)
member.summary: Extracts the tenant ID from the provided authority URI and overrides the tenant ID specified in the authority at the application level. This operation preserves the authority host (environment) provided to the application builder. If an authority was not provided to the application builder, this method will replace the tenant ID in the default authority - `https://login.microsoftonline.com/common`.
member.param: URI from which to extract the tenant ID
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.
member.exception: Thrown if is null or an empty string.
Thrown if is not well-formatted (for example, has spaces).
Thrown in general exception scenarios (for example if the application was configured with an authority that does not allow tenants).
member.remarks: The tenant should be more restrictive than the one configured at the application level, e.g. don't use "common". Does not affect authority validation, which is specified at the application level.

member.param: Authority URL for an ADFS server.
Whether the authority should be validated against the server metadata.
member.remarks: MSAL.NET supports ADFS 2019 or later.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.

members.member:
Adds a known authority corresponding to an Azure AD B2C policy. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-b2c-specificities
Azure AD B2C authority, including the B2C policy (for instance "https://fabrikamb2c.b2clogin.com/tfp/{Tenant}/{policy}). The builder to chain the .With methods.

member.summary: Base class for parameter builders common to public client application and confidential client application token acquisition operations

member.summary: Base class for confidential client application token request builders

member.summary: Validates the parameters of the AcquireToken operation.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractConfidentialClientAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithProofOfPossession(Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration)
member.summary: Modifies the token acquisition request so that the acquired token is a Proof-of-Possession token (PoP), rather than a Bearer token. PoP tokens are similar to Bearer tokens, but are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL can manage on Windows. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-pop
member.param: Configuration properties used to construct a Proof-of-Possession request.
member.returns: The builder.

item.description: The PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, more specifically to the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) and to the Uri (path and query, but not query parameters).
MSAL creates, reads and stores a key in memory that will be cycled every 8 hours.
This is an experimental API. The method signature may change in the future without involving a major version upgrade.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractConfidentialClientAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithSignedHttpRequestProofOfPossession(Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration)
member.summary: Modifies the request to acquire a Signed HTTP Request (SHR) Proof-of-Possession (PoP) token, rather than a Bearer. SHR PoP tokens are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL manages on Windows. SHR PoP tokens are different from mTLS PoP tokens, which are used for Mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication. See for details.
member.param: Configuration properties used to construct a Proof-of-Possession request.
member.returns: The builder.

item.description: The SHR PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, specifically to the HTTP method (for example, `GET` or `POST`) and to the URI path and query, excluding query parameters.
MSAL creates, reads, and stores a key in memory that will be cycled every 8 hours.
This is an experimental API. The method signature may change in the future without involving a major version upgrade.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenByAuthorizationCodeParameterBuilder
member.summary: Builder for AcquireTokenByAuthorizationCode

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenByAuthorizationCodeParameterBuilder.WithSendX5C(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: true if the x5c should be sent. Otherwise false. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenByAuthorizationCodeParameterBuilder.WithPkceCodeVerifier(System.String)
member.summary: Used to secure authorization code grant via Proof of Key for Code Exchange (PKCE). See (https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7636) for more details.
member.param: A dynamically created cryptographically random key used to provide proof of possession for the authorization code.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenByRefreshTokenParameterBuilder.WithSendX5C(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: true if the x5c should be sent. Otherwise false. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenByUsernamePasswordParameterBuilder.WithProofOfPossession(System.String,System.Net.Http.HttpMethod,System.Uri)
member.summary: Modifies the token acquisition request so that the acquired token is a Proof-of-Possession token (PoP), rather than a Bearer token. PoP tokens are similar to Bearer tokens, but are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL can manage on Windows. Note that only the host and path parts of the request URI will be bound. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-pop
member.param: Nonce of the protected resource (RP) which will be published as part of the WWWAuthenticate header associated with a 401 HTTP response or as part of the AuthorityInfo header associated with 200 response. Set it here to make it part of the Signed HTTP Request part of the POP token.
The HTTP method ("GET", "POST" etc.) method that will be bound to the token. If set to null, the PoP token will not be bound to the method. Corresponds to the "m" part of the a signed HTTP request.
The URI to bind the signed HTTP request to.
member.returns: The builder.

item.description: An Authentication header is automatically added to the request.
The PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, more specifically to the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) and to the Uri (path and query, but not query parameters).
Broker is required to use Proof-of-Possession on public clients.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder
member.summary: Builder for AcquireTokenForClient (used in client credential flows, in daemon applications). See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-credentials

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder.WithForceRefresh(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Specifies if the client application should ignore access tokens when reading the token cache. New tokens will still be written to the application token cache. By default the token is taken from the application token cache (forceRefresh=false)
member.param: If true, the request will ignore cached access tokens on read, but will still write them to the cache once obtained from the identity provider. The default is false
member.remarks: Do not use this flag except in well understood cases. Identity providers will throttle clients that make too many similar token requests.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder.WithSendX5C(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: true if the x5c should be sent. Otherwise false. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder.WithMtlsProofOfPossession
member.summary: Specifies that the certificate provided will be used for PoP tokens with mTLS (Mutual TLS) authentication. For more information, refer to the Proof-of-Possession documentation.
member.returns: The current instance of to enable method chaining.

member.summary: Please use WithAzureRegion on the ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder object

member.summary: Please use WithAzureRegion on the ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder object

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder.WithAttributes(System.String)
member.summary: Specifies an identity attribute to include in the token request. The attribute values will be returned as a claim in the token called "xms_attr" in the access token claims. This is typically used with FMI (Federated Managed Identity) scenarios. Example attribute json: "{\"sg1\":\"0000-00000-0001\",\"sg2\":[\"0000-00000-0002\",\"0000-00000-0003\",\"0000-00000-0004\"]}"
member.param: The attribute value to include in the request
member.returns: The builder to chain method calls
member.remarks: The attribute value is included in the cache key, so different attribute values will result in different cache entries. This ensures that tokens with different attributes are not confused with each other.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForClientParameterBuilder.WithFmiPath(System.String)
member.summary: Adds an fmi_path parameter to the request. It modifies the subject of the token.

members.member: for a comment inside this function for AzureRegion.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForManagedIdentityParameterBuilder
member.summary: Builder for AcquireTokenForManagedIdentity (used to get token for managed identities). See https://aka.ms/msal-net-managed-identity

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForManagedIdentityParameterBuilder.WithForceRefresh(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Specifies if the client application should ignore access tokens when reading the token cache. New tokens will still be written to the application token cache. By default the token is taken from the application token cache (forceRefresh=false)
member.param: If true, the request will ignore cached access tokens on read, but will still write them to the cache once obtained from the Identity Provider. The default is false
member.remarks: Do not use this flag except in well understood cases. Identity Providers will throttle clients that issue too many similar token requests.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenForManagedIdentityParameterBuilder.WithClaims(System.String)
member.summary: Adds a claims challenge to the token request. The SDK will bypass the token cache when a claims challenge is specified. Retry the token acquisition, and use this value in the method. A claims challenge typically arises when calling the protected downstream API, for example when the tenant administrator revokes credentials. Apps are required to look for a 401 Unauthorized response from the protected api and to parse the WWW-Authenticate response header in order to extract the claims. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-claim-challenge for details.
member.param: A string with one or multiple claims.
member.returns: The builder to chain .With methods.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenInteractiveParameterBuilder
member.summary: Builder for an Interactive token request. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-acquire-token-interactively

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenInteractiveParameterBuilder.WithUseEmbeddedWebView(System.Boolean)
member.summary:
Specifies if the public client application should used an embedded web browser or the system default browser. If the broker (WAM, Authenticator, Company Portal) is configured, this setting is only used when the broker is not installed.
On .NET, including net8-windows, app developers must reference Microsoft.Identity.Client.Desktop and call PublicClientApplicationBuilder.WithDesktopFeatures() to enable the embedded web browser.
member.param: If true, will use an embedded web browser, otherwise will attempt to use a system web browser. The default depends on the platform: false for iOS and Android, and true for .NET Framework
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenInteractiveParameterBuilder.WithProofOfPossession(System.String,System.Net.Http.HttpMethod,System.Uri)
member.summary: Modifies the token acquisition request so that the acquired token is a Proof-of-Possession token (PoP), rather than a Bearer token. PoP tokens are similar to Bearer tokens, but are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL can manage on Windows. Note that only the host and path parts of the request URI will be bound. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-pop
member.param: Nonce of the protected resource which will be published as part of the WWW-Authenticate header associated with a 401 HTTP response or as part of the AuthorityInfo header associated with 200 response. Set it here to make it part of the Signed HTTP Request part of the PoP token.
The HTTP method ("GET", "POST" etc.) method that will be bound to the token. If set to null, the PoP token will not be bound to the method. Corresponds to the "m" part of the a signed HTTP request.
The URI to bind the signed HTTP request to.
member.returns: The builder.

item.description: An Authentication header is automatically added to the request.
The PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, more specifically to the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) and to the Uri (path and query, but not query parameters).
Broker is required to use Proof-of-Possession on public clients.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenOnBehalfOfParameterBuilder
member.summary: Builder for AcquireTokenOnBehalfOf (OBO flow) See https://aka.ms/msal-net-on-behalf-of

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenOnBehalfOfParameterBuilder.WithCacheKey(System.String)
member.summary: Specifies a key by which to look up the token in the cache instead of searching by an assertion.
member.param: Key by which to look up the token in the cache
member.returns: A builder enabling you to add optional parameters before executing the token request

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenOnBehalfOfParameterBuilder.WithSendX5C(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: true if the x5c should be sent. Otherwise false. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenOnBehalfOfParameterBuilder.WithForceRefresh(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Specifies if the client application should ignore access tokens when reading the token cache. New tokens will still be written to the token cache. By default the token is taken from the the user token cache (forceRefresh=false)
member.param: If true, ignore any access token in the user token cache and attempt to acquire new access token using the refresh token for the account if one is available. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods
member.remarks: Do not use this flag except in well understood cases. Identity Providers will throttle clients that issue too many similar token requests.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenSilentParameterBuilder.WithForceRefresh(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Specifies if the client application should ignore access tokens when reading the token cache. Refresh tokens will still be used. Any new tokens from the Identity Provider will still be written to the token cache. By default the token is taken from the the user token cache (forceRefresh=false)
member.param: If true, ignore any access token in the user token cache and attempt to acquire new access token using the refresh token for the account if one is available. This can be useful in the case when the application developer wants to make sure that conditional access policies are applied immediately, rather than after the expiration of the access token. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods
member.remarks: Avoid unnecessarily setting to true true in order to avoid negatively affecting the performance of your application

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenSilentParameterBuilder.WithSendX5C(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: true if the x5c should be sent. Otherwise false. The default is false
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenSilentParameterBuilder.WithProofOfPossession(Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration)
member.summary: Modifies the token acquisition request so that the acquired token is a Proof-of-Possession token (PoP), rather than a Bearer token. PoP tokens are similar to Bearer tokens, but are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL can manage on Windows. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-pop
member.param: Configuration properties used to construct a Proof-of-Possession request.

item.description: An Authentication header is automatically added to the request.
The PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, more specifically to the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) and to the Uri (path and query, but not query parameters).
MSAL creates, reads and stores a key in memory that will be cycled every 8 hours.
This is an experimental API. The method signature may change in the future without involving a major version upgrade.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AcquireTokenSilentParameterBuilder.WithProofOfPossession(System.String,System.Net.Http.HttpMethod,System.Uri)

PoP tokens are similar to Bearer tokens, but are bound to the HTTP request and to a cryptographic key, which MSAL can manage on Windows. Note that only the host and path parts of the request URI will be bound. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-pop
member.param: Nonce of the protected resource (RP) which will be published as part of the WWWAuthenticate header associated with a 401 HTTP response or as part of the AuthorityInfo header associated with 200 response. Set it here to make it part of the Signed HTTP Request part of the POP token.
The HTTP method ("GET", "POST" etc.) method that will be bound to the token. If set to null, the PoP token will not be bound to the method. Corresponds to the "m" part of the a signed HTTP request.
The URI to bind the signed HTTP request to.
member.returns: The builder.

item.description: An Authentication header is automatically added to the request.
The PoP token is bound to the HTTP request, more specifically to the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) and to the Uri (path and query, but not query parameters).
MSAL creates, reads and stores a key in memory that will be cycled every 8 hours.
On confidential clients, this is an experimental API. The method signature may change in the future without involving a major version upgrade.
Broker is required to use Proof-of-Possession on public clients.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseAbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.ExecuteAsync(System.Threading.CancellationToken)
member.summary: Executes the Token request asynchronously, with a possibility of cancelling the asynchronous method.
member.param: Cancellation token. See
member.returns: Authentication result containing a token for the requested scopes and parameters set in the builder.

remarks.para: Cancellation is not guaranteed, it is best effort. If the operation reaches a point of no return, e.g. tokens are acquired and written to the cache, the task will complete even if cancellation was requested. Do not rely on cancellation tokens for strong consistency.
To learn more about potential exceptions thrown by the function, refer to Exceptions in MSAL.NET.

member.summary: Executes the Token request asynchronously.

member.param: Correlation id of the authentication request.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseAbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithExtraQueryParameters(System.Collections.Generic.Dictionary{System.String,System.String})
member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request.
member.param: This parameter will be appended as is to the query string in the HTTP authentication request to the authority as a string of segments of the form key=value separated by an ampersand character. The parameter can be null.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseAbstractAcquireTokenParameterBuilder`1.WithExtraQueryParameters(System.Collections.Generic.IDictionary{System.String,System.ValueTuple{System.String,System.Boolean}})
member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request with control over which parameters are included in the cache key
member.param: This parameter will be appended as is to the query string in the HTTP authentication request to the authority, and merged with those added to the application-level WithExtraQueryParameters API. Each dictionary entry maps a parameter name to a tuple containing: - Value: The parameter value that will be appended to the query string - IncludeInCacheKey: Whether this parameter should be included when computing the token's cache key. To help ensure the correct token is returned from the cache, IncludeInCacheKey should be true if the parameter affects token content or validity (e.g., resource-specific claims or parameters). The parameter can be null.
member.returns: The builder to chain .With methods.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithInstanceDicoveryMetadata(System.String)
member.summary:
Allows developers to configure their own valid authorities. A json string similar to https://aka.ms/aad-instance-discovery should be provided. MSAL uses this information to:
Call REST APIs on the environment specified in the preferred_network
Identify an environment under which to save tokens and accounts in the cache
Use the environment aliases to match tokens issued to other authorities
For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-custom-instance-metadata
member.remarks: Developers take responsibility for authority validation if they use this method. Should not be used when the authority is not know in advance. Has no effect on ADFS or B2C authorities, only for AAD authorities

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithInstanceDiscoveryMetadata(System.String)
member.summary:
Allows developers to configure their own valid authorities. A json string similar to https://aka.ms/aad-instance-discovery should be provided. MSAL uses this information to:
Call REST APIs on the environment specified in the preferred_network
Identify an environment under which to save tokens and accounts in the cache
Use the environment aliases to match tokens issued to other authorities
For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-custom-instance-metadata
member.remarks: Developers take responsibility for authority validation if they use this method. Should not be used when the authority is not known in advance. Has no effect on ADFS or B2C authorities, only for AAD authorities

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithInstanceDicoveryMetadata(System.Uri)
member.summary:
Lets an organization setup their own service to handle instance discovery, which enables better caching for microservice/service environments. A Uri that returns a response similar to https://aka.ms/aad-instance-discovery should be provided. MSAL uses this information to:
Call REST APIs on the environment specified in the preferred_network
Identify an environment under which to save tokens and accounts in the cache
Use the environment aliases to match tokens issued to other authorities
For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-custom-instance-metadata
member.remarks: Developers take responsibility for authority validation if they use this method. Should not be used when the authority is not know in advance. Has no effect on ADFS or B2C authorities, only for AAD authorities

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithCacheOptions(Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions)
member.summary:
Options for MSAL token caches.
MSAL maintains a token cache internally in memory. By default, this cache object is part of each instance of or . This method allows customization of the in-memory token cache of MSAL.
MSAL's memory cache is different than token cache serialization. Cache serialization pulls the tokens from a cache (e.g. Redis, Cosmos, or a file on disk), where they are stored in JSON format, into MSAL's internal memory cache. Memory cache operations do not involve JSON operations.
External cache serialization remains the recommended way to handle desktop apps, web site and web APIs, as it provides persistence. These options do not currently control external cache serialization.
Detailed guidance for each application type and platform: https://aka.ms/msal-net-token-cache-serialization
member.param: Options for the internal MSAL token caches.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithLegacyCacheCompatibility(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Enables legacy ADAL cache serialization and deserialization.
member.param: Enable legacy ADAL cache compatibility.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods.
member.remarks: ADAL is a previous legacy generation of MSAL.NET authentication library. If you don't use .WithLegacyCacheCompatibility(false), then by default, the ADAL cache is used (along with MSAL cache). true flag is only needed for specific migration scenarios from ADAL.NET to MSAL.NET when both library versions are running side-by-side. To improve performance add .WithLegacyCacheCompatibility(false) unless you care about migration scenarios.

member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithExtraQueryParameters(System.String)
member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request
member.param: This parameter will be appended as is to the query string in the HTTP authentication request to the authority. The string needs to be properly URL-encoded and ready to send as a string of segments of the form key=value separated by an ampersand character.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithExtraQueryParameters(System.Collections.Generic.IDictionary{System.String,System.ValueTuple{System.String,System.Boolean}})
member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request with control over which parameters are included in the cache key
member.param: This parameter will be appended as is to the query string in the HTTP authentication request to the authority, and merged with those added to the request-level WithExtraQueryParameters API. Each dictionary entry maps a parameter name to a tuple containing: - Value: The parameter value that will be appended to the query string - IncludeInCacheKey: Whether this parameter should be included when computing the token's cache key. To help ensure the correct token is returned from the cache, IncludeInCacheKey should be true if the parameter affects token content or validity (e.g., resource-specific claims or parameters). The parameter can be null.
member.returns: The builder to chain .With methods.

member.summary: Adds a known Azure AD authority to the application to sign-in users specifying the full authority URI. See Application configuration options.
member.param:
URI of the authority from which MSAL.NET will acquire the tokens. Authority endpoints for the Azure public Cloud are:
https://login.microsoftonline.com/tenant/ where tenant is the tenant ID of the Azure AD tenant or a domain associated with this Azure AD tenant, in order to sign-in users of a specific organization only
https://login.microsoftonline.com/common/ to sign-in users with any work and school accounts or personal Microsoft accounts
https://login.microsoftonline.com/organizations/ to sign-in users with any work and school accounts
https://login.microsoftonline.com/consumers/ to sign-in users with only personal Microsoft accounts (live)
Note that this setting needs to be consistent with what is declared in the application registration portal
Whether the authority should be validated against the server metadata.
member.exception: Thrown if is null or empty.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ApplicationOptions.RedirectUri
member.summary:
This redirect URI needs to be registered in the app registration. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-register-app for details on which redirect URIs are defined by default by MSAL.NET and how to register them. Also use: which provides a good default for public client applications for all platforms.
For web apps and web APIs, the redirect URI is computed from the URL where the application is running (for instance, baseUrl//signin-oidc for ASP.NET Core web apps).
For daemon applications (confidential client applications using only the Client Credential flow that is calling AcquireTokenForClient), no reply URI is needed.
member.remarks: This is especially important when you deploy an application that you have initially tested locally; you then need to add the reply URL of the deployed application in the application registration portal

member.summary: This is extension for AuthorityInfo

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AuthorityInfo.AuthorityInfoHelper.CreateAuthorityForRequestAsync(Microsoft.Identity.Client.Internal.RequestContext,Microsoft.Identity.Client.AuthorityInfo,Microsoft.Identity.Client.IAccount)
member.summary:
Figures out the authority based on the authority from the config and the authority from the request, and optionally the homeAccountTenantId, which has an impact on AcquireTokenSilent If the request authority is consumers, organizations, or common, it should just be set an the app level. The algorithm is:
1. If there is no request authority (i.e. no authority override), use the config authority. 1.1. For AAD, if the config authority is "common" etc, try to use the tenanted version with the home account tenant ID 2. If there is a request authority, try to use it. 2.1. If the request authority is not "common", then use it 2.2 If the request authority is "common", ignore it, and use 1.1
Special cases:
- if the authority is not defined at the application level and the request level is not AAD, use the request authority - if the authority is defined at app level, and the request level authority is of different type, throw an exception
- if the intended authority is consumers, please define it at the app level and not at the request level. known issue: https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-dotnet/issues/2929

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseAbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithHttpClientFactory(Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalHttpClientFactory)
member.summary: Uses a specific to communicate with the IdP. This enables advanced scenarios such as setting a proxy, or setting the Agent.
member.param: HTTP client factory
member.remarks: MSAL does not guarantee that it will not modify the HttpClient, for example by adding new headers. Prior to the changes needed in order to make MSAL's httpClients thread safe (https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-dotnet/pull/2046/files), the httpClient had the possibility of throwing an exception stating "Properties can only be modified before sending the first request". MSAL's httpClient will no longer throw this exception after 4.19.0 (https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-dotnet/releases/tag/4.19.0) see (https://aka.ms/msal-httpclient-info) for more information.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseAbstractApplicationBuilder`1.WithHttpClientFactory(Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalHttpClientFactory,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Uses a specific to communicate with the IdP. This enables advanced scenarios such as setting a proxy, or setting the Agent.
member.param: HTTP client factory

Configures MSAL to retry on 5xx server errors. When enabled (on by default), MSAL will wait 1 second after receiving a 5xx error and then retry the http request again. When disabled, the developer will be responsible for configuring their own retry policy in their custom IMsalHttpClientFactory.
member.remarks: MSAL does not guarantee that it will not modify the HttpClient, for example by adding new headers. Prior to the changes needed in order to make MSAL's httpClients thread safe (https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-dotnet/pull/2046/files), the httpClient had the possibility of throwing an exception stating "Properties can only be modified before sending the first request". MSAL's httpClient will no longer throw this exception after 4.19.0 (https://github.com/AzureAD/microsoft-authentication-library-for-dotnet/releases/tag/4.19.0) see (https://aka.ms/msal-httpclient-info) for more information. If you only want to configure the retryOnceOn5xx parameter, set httpClientFactory to null and MSAL will use the default http client.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

members.member:
Sets the logging callback. For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-logging
Desired level of logging. The default is LogLevel.Info Boolean used to enable/disable logging of Personally Identifiable Information (PII). PII logs are never written to default outputs like Console, Logcat or NSLog Default is set to false, which ensures that your application is compliant with GDPR. You can set it to true for advanced debugging requiring PII If both WithLogging apis are set, the other one will override the this one
Flag to enable/disable logging to platform defaults. In Desktop, Event Tracing is used. In iOS, NSLog is used. In android, Logcat is used. The default value is false
The builder to chain the .With methods is thrown if the loggingCallback was already set on the application builder

Boolean used to enable/disable logging of Personally Identifiable Information (PII). PII logs are never written to default outputs like Console, Logcat or NSLog Default is set to false, which ensures that your application is compliant with GDPR. You can set it to true for advanced debugging requiring PII If both WithLogging apis are set, this one will override the other

Desired level of logging. The default is LogLevel.Info Boolean used to enable/disable logging of Personally Identifiable Information (PII). PII logs are never written to default outputs like Console, Logcat or NSLog Default is set to false, which ensures that your application is compliant with GDPR. You can set it to true for advanced debugging requiring PII
Flag to enable/disable logging to platform defaults. In Desktop, Event Tracing is used. In iOS, NSLog is used. In android, logcat is used. The default value is false
The builder to chain the .With methods is thrown if the loggingCallback was already set on the application builder by calling

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseApplicationOptions
member.summary: Base class for options objects with string values loadable from a configuration file (for instance a JSON file, as in an asp.net configuration scenario) See https://aka.ms/msal-net-application-configuration See also derived classes

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseApplicationOptions.LogLevel
member.summary: Enables you to configure the level of logging you want. The default value is . Setting it to will only get errors Setting it to will get errors and warning, etc.. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-logging

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseApplicationOptions.EnablePiiLogging
member.summary: Flag to enable/disable logging of Personally Identifiable Information (PII). PII logs are never written to default outputs like Console, Logcat or NSLog Default is set to false, which ensures that your application is compliant with GDPR. You can set it to true for advanced debugging requiring PII. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-logging

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.BaseApplicationOptions.IsDefaultPlatformLoggingEnabled
member.summary: Flag to enable/disable logging to platform defaults. In Desktop, Event Tracing is used. In iOS, NSLog is used. In Android, logcat is used. The default value is false. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-logging

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions
member.summary: Options for MSAL token caches.
member.remarks: Detailed cache guidance for each application type and platform, including L2 options: https://aka.ms/msal-net-token-cache-serialization

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions.EnableSharedCacheOptions
member.summary: Recommended options for using a static cache.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions.#ctor
member.summary: Constructor for the options with default values.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions.#ctor(System.Boolean)
member.summary: Constructor
member.param: Set to true to share the cache between all ClientApplication objects. The cache becomes static. for a detailed description.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.CacheOptions.UseSharedCache
member.summary: Share the cache between all ClientApplication objects. The cache becomes static. Defaults to false.
member.remarks: Recommended only for client credentials flow (service to service communication). Web apps and Web APIs should use external token caching (Redis, Cosmos etc.) for scaling purposes. Desktop apps should encrypt and persist their token cache to disk, to avoid losing tokens when app restarts. ADAL used a static cache by default.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.CertificateOptions
member.summary: Represents configuration options for certificate handling or management.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.CertificateOptions.SendX5C
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating whether the X.509 certificate chain (x5c) should be included in the token request.
member.remarks: Set this property to to include X5C in the token request otherwise, set it to .

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.CertificateOptions.AssociateTokensWithCertificate
member.summary: Gets or sets a value indicating if the application tokens acquired from Azure AD are associated with the certificate serial number. This property when set, allow you to associate the tokens acquired from Azure AD with the certificate serial number. This can be used to partition the cache by certificate. Tokens acquired with one certificate will not be accessible to another certificate with a different serial number. Set this property to to indicate that the tokens acquired from Azure AD are associated with the certificate serial number, by default it is set to />

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.ManagedIdentityId
member.summary: Class to store configuration for a managed identity enabled on a resource. For a system assigned managed identity use ManagedIdentityId.SystemAssigned. For user assigned managed identity use ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedClientId("clientId") or ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedResourceId("resourceId") or ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedObjectId("objectid"). For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-managed-identity

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.ManagedIdentityId.UserAssignedId
member.summary: Gets the identifier for a user-assigned managed identity.
member.remarks: This property holds the unique identifier of the user-assigned managed identity. It can be a client ID, a resource ID, or an object ID, depending on how the managed identity is configured.
member.value: The identifier string of the user-assigned managed identity.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.ManagedIdentityId.IdType
member.summary: Gets the type of identifier used for the managed identity.
member.remarks: This property indicates the type of the managed identity identifier, which can be either a client ID, a resource ID, or an object ID.
member.value: The enumeration value representing the managed identity identifier type.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.ManagedIdentityId.IsUserAssigned
member.summary: Gets a value indicating whether the managed identity is user-assigned.
member.remarks: This property is true if the managed identity is user-assigned, and false if it is system-assigned.
member.value: True if the managed identity is user-assigned; otherwise, false.

member.summary: Create an instance of ManagedIdentityId for a system assigned managed identity.

member.returns: Instance of ManagedIdentityId.

member.summary: Create an instance of ManagedIdentityId for a user assigned managed identity from a resource id.
member.param: Resource id of the user assigned managed identity assigned to the azure resource.
member.returns: Instance of ManagedIdentityId.

member.summary: Create an instance of ManagedIdentityId for a user assigned managed identity from an object id.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.MsalClientType
member.summary: Enum to represent the type of MSAL application.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration
member.summary: Details about the HTTP request and configuration properties used to construct a proof of possession request.
member.remarks: POP tokens are signed by the process making the request. By default, MSAL will generate a key in memory. To use a hardware key or an external key, implement .

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.#ctor
member.summary: Creates a configuration using the default key management - an RSA key will be created in memory and rotated every 8h. Uses , etc. to control which elements of the request should be included in the POP token.
member.remarks: See https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-signed-http-request-03#page-3 for details about signed HTTP requests.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.#ctor(System.Net.Http.HttpRequestMessage)
member.summary: Creates a configuration using the default key management, and which binds all the details of the HttpRequestMessage.
member.remarks: Currently only the HttpMethod (m), UrlHost (u) and UrlPath (p) are used to create the signed HTTP request - see https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-signed-http-request-03#page-3

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.#ctor(System.Uri)
member.summary: Creates a configuration using the default key management, and which binds only the Uri part of the HTTP request.
member.remarks: The UrlHost (u) and UrlPath (p) are used to create the signed HTTP request - see https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-signed-http-request-03#page-3

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.HttpMethod
member.summary: The HTTP method ("GET", "POST" etc.) method that will be bound to the token. Leave null and the POP token will not be bound to the method. Corresponds to the "m" part of the a signed HTTP request. Optional.
member.remarks: See https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-ietf-oauth-signed-http-request-03#section-3

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.HttpHost
member.summary: The URL host of the protected API. The "u" part of a signed HTTP request. This MAY include the port separated from the host by a colon in host:port format. Optional.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.HttpPath
member.summary: The "p" part of the signed HTTP request.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.PopCryptoProvider
member.summary: An extensibility point that allows developers to define their own key management. Leave null and MSAL will use a default implementation, which generates an RSA key pair in memory and refreshes it every 8 hours. Important note: if you want to change the key (e.g. rotate the key), you should create a new instance of this object, as MSAL.NET will keep a thumbprint of keys in memory.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.Nonce
member.summary: If the protected resource (RP) requires use of a special nonce, they will publish it as part of the WWWAuthenticate header associated with a 401 HTTP response or as part of the AuthorityInfo header associated with 200 response. Set it here to make it part of the Signed HTTP Request part of the POP token.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.PoPAuthenticationConfiguration.SignHttpRequest
member.summary: Allows app developers to bypass the creation of the SignedHttpRequest envelope by setting this property to false. App developers can use a package like Microsoft.IdentityModel.Protocols.SignedHttpRequest to later create and sign the envelope.
member.remarks: If set to false, you do not need to implement the method when using custom keys.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ClientSignedAssertion
member.summary: Container returned from WithClientAssertion.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ClientSignedAssertion.Assertion
member.summary: Represents the client assertion (JWT) and optional mutual‑TLS binding certificate returned by the clientAssertionProvider callback supplied to .
member.remarks:
MSAL forwards to the token endpoint as the client_assertion parameter. When mutual‑TLS Proof‑of‑Possession (PoP) is enabled on the application and a is provided, MSAL sets client_assertion_type to urn:ietf:params:oauth:client-assertion-type:jwt-pop; otherwise it uses jwt-bearer.
Guidance on constructing the client assertion (required claims, audience, and lifetime) is available at aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion. The assertion is created by your callback; MSAL does not modify or re‑sign it. **Note:** It is up to the caller to cache the assertion and certificate if reuse is desired.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ClientSignedAssertion.TokenBindingCertificate
member.summary: Optional. Certificate used to bind the client assertion for mutual‑TLS Proof‑of‑Possession (PoP).
member.remarks: Provide a value only when PoP is enabled on the application. The certificate should include an accessible private key. If null, MSAL treats the assertion as a bearer assertion and uses client_assertion_type=jwt-bearer.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.CreateWithApplicationOptions(Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationOptions)
member.summary: Constructor of a ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder from application configuration options. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-application-configuration
member.param: Confidential client applications configuration options
member.returns: A from which to set more parameters, and to create a confidential client application instance

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.Create(System.String)
member.summary: Creates a ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder from a clientID. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-application-configuration
member.param: Client ID (also known as App ID) of the application as registered in the application registration portal (https://aka.ms/msal-net-register-app)/.
member.returns: A from which to set more parameters, and to create a confidential client application instance

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithCertificate(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2)
member.summary: Sets the certificate associated with the application.
member.param: The X509 certificate used as credentials to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD.
member.remarks: You should use certificates with a private key size of at least 2048 bytes. Future versions of this library might reject certificates with smaller keys. Does not send the certificate (as x5c parameter) with the request by default.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithCertificate(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Sets the certificate associated with the application. Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: The X509 certificate used as credentials to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD.
To send X5C with every request or not. The default is false
member.remarks: You should use certificates with a private key size of at least 2048 bytes. Future versions of this library might reject certificates with smaller keys.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithCertificate(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2,Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.CertificateOptions)
member.summary: Sets the certificate associated with the application. Applicable to first-party applications only, this method also allows to specify if the x5c claim should be sent to Azure AD. Sending the x5c enables application developers to achieve easy certificate roll-over in Azure AD: this method will send the certificate chain to Azure AD along with the token request, so that Azure AD can use it to validate the subject name based on a trusted issuer policy. This saves the application admin from the need to explicitly manage the certificate rollover (either via portal or PowerShell/CLI operation). For details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-sni
member.param: The X509 certificate used as credentials to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD.
Configuration options for certificate handling. See for more information.
member.remarks: You should use certificates with a private key size of at least 2048 bytes. Future versions of this library might reject certificates with smaller keys.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientClaims(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2,System.Collections.Generic.IDictionary{System.String,System.String},System.Boolean)
member.summary: Sets the certificate associated with the application along with the specific claims to sign. By default, this will merge the with the default required set of claims needed for authentication. If is set to false, you will need to provide the required default claims. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion
member.param: The X509 certificate used as credentials to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD.
The claims to be signed by the provided certificate.
Determines whether or not to merge with the default claims required for authentication.
member.remarks: You should use certificates with a private key size of at least 2048 bytes. Future versions of this library might reject certificates with smaller keys. Does not send the certificate (as x5c parameter) with the request by default.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientClaims(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2,System.Collections.Generic.IDictionary{System.String,System.String},System.Boolean,System.Boolean)
member.summary: Sets the certificate associated with the application along with the specific claims to sign. By default, this will merge the with the default required set of claims needed for authentication. If is set to false, you will need to provide the required default claims. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion
member.param: The X509 certificate used as credentials to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD.
The claims to be signed by the provided certificate.
Determines whether or not to merge with the default claims required for authentication.
To send X5C with every request or not.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientSecret(System.String)
member.summary: Sets the application secret
member.param: Secret string previously shared with AAD at application registration to prove the identity of the application (the client) requesting the tokens

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertion(System.String)
member.summary: Sets the application client assertion. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion. This will create an assertion that will be held within the client application's memory for the duration of the client. You can use to set a delegate that will be executed for each authentication request. This will allow you to update the client assertion used by the client application once the assertion expires.
member.param: The client assertion used to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD. This is a Base-64 encoded JWT.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertion(System.Func{System.String})
member.summary: Configures a delegate that creates a client assertion. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion
member.param: delegate computing the client assertion used to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD. This is a delegate that computes a Base-64 encoded JWT for each authentication call.
member.returns: The ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder to chain more .With methods
member.remarks: Callers can use this mechanism to cache their assertions

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertion(System.Func{System.Threading.CancellationToken,System.Threading.Tasks.Task{System.String}})
member.summary: Configures an async delegate that creates a client assertion. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion
member.param: An async delegate computing the client assertion used to prove the identity of the application to Azure AD. This is a delegate that computes a Base-64 encoded JWT for each authentication call.
member.returns: The ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder to chain more .With methods
member.remarks: Callers can use this mechanism to cache their assertions

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertion(System.Func{Microsoft.Identity.Client.AssertionRequestOptions,System.Threading.Tasks.Task{System.String}})
member.summary: Configures an async delegate that creates a client assertion. The delegate is invoked only when a token cannot be retrieved from the cache. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-client-assertion
member.param: An async delegate that returns the client assertion. Assertion lifetime is the responsibility of the caller.
member.returns: The ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder to chain more .With methods
member.remarks: Callers can use this mechanism to cache their assertions

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertion(System.Func{Microsoft.Identity.Client.AssertionRequestOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken,System.Threading.Tasks.Task{Microsoft.Identity.Client.ClientSignedAssertion}})
member.summary: Configures the client application to use a client assertion for authentication.
member.remarks: This method allows the client application to authenticate using a custom client assertion, which can be useful in scenarios where the assertion needs to be dynamically generated or retrieved.
member.param: A delegate that asynchronously provides an based on the given and . This delegate must not be .
member.returns: The instance configured with the specified client assertion.
member.exception: Thrown if is .

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertionInternal(System.Func{Microsoft.Identity.Client.AssertionRequestOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken,System.Threading.Tasks.Task{Microsoft.Identity.Client.ClientSignedAssertion}})
member.summary: Internal helper to set the client assertion provider.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithClientAssertionInternal(System.Func{Microsoft.Identity.Client.AssertionRequestOptions,System.Threading.CancellationToken,System.Threading.Tasks.Task{System.String}})
member.summary: Internal helper to set the client assertion provider that returns a string.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithAzureRegion(System.String)
member.summary: Instructs MSAL to use an Azure regional token service. This feature is currently available to first-party applications only.
member.param: Either the string with the region (preferred) or use and MSAL will attempt to auto-detect the region.
member.remarks: The region value should be a short region name for the region where the service is deployed. For example, "centralus" is short name for region Central US. Currently only tokens for the client credential flow can be obtained from the regional service. Requires configuration at the tenant level. Auto-detection works on a limited number of Azure artifacts (VMs, Azure functions). If auto-detection fails, the non-regional endpoint will be used. If a specific region was provided and the token web request failed, verify that the region name is valid. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-region-discovery for more details.
member.returns: The builder to chain the .With methods

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithCacheSynchronization(System.Boolean)
member.summary: When set to true, MSAL will lock cache access at the level, i.e. the block of code between BeforeAccessAsync and AfterAccessAsync callbacks will be synchronized. Apps can set this flag to false to enable an optimistic cache locking strategy, which may result in better performance at the cost of cache consistency. Setting this flag to false is only recommended for apps which create a new per request.
member.remarks: This flag is true by default. The default behavior is recommended.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithGenericAuthority(System.String)
member.summary: Call instead.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithOidcAuthority(System.String)
member.summary: Adds a known authority corresponding to a generic OpenIdConnect Identity Provider. MSAL will append ".well-known/openid-configuration" to the authority and retrieve the OIDC metadata from there, to figure out the endpoints. See https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0.html#Terminology
member.remarks: Do not use this method with Entra ID authorities (e.g. https://login.microsoftonline.com/common). Use WithAuthority(string) instead.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationBuilder.WithTelemetryClient(Microsoft.IdentityModel.Abstractions.ITelemetryClient[])
member.summary: This method is obsolete. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-telemetry

member.summary: Builds an instance of from the parameters set in the .
member.exception: Thrown when errors occur locally in the library itself (for example, because of incorrect configuration).
member.returns: An instance of

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationOptions
member.summary: Configuration options for a confidential client application (web app / web API / daemon app). See https://aka.ms/msal-net/application-configuration

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationOptions.ClientSecret
member.summary: Client secret for the confidential client application. This secret (application password) is provided by the application registration portal, or provided to Azure AD during the application registration with PowerShell AzureAD, PowerShell AzureRM, or Azure CLI.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationOptions.AzureRegion
member.summary: Instructs MSAL.NET to use an Azure regional token service. This setting should be set to either the string with the region (preferred) or to "TryAutoDetect" and MSAL.NET will attempt to auto-detect the region.
member.remarks: Region names as per Region class documentation. Not all auth flows can use the regional token service. Service To Service (client credential flow) tokens can be obtained from the regional service. Requires configuration at the tenant level. Auto-detection works on a limited number of Azure artifacts (VMs, Azure functions). If auto-detection fails, the non-regional endpoint will be used. If an invalid region name is provided, the non-regional endpoint MIGHT be used or the token request MIGHT fail. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-region-discovery for more details.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ConfidentialClientApplicationOptions.EnableCacheSynchronization
member.summary: When set to true, MSAL will lock cache access at the level, i.e. the block of code between BeforeAccessAsync and AfterAccessAsync callbacks will be synchronized. Apps can set this flag to false to enable an optimistic cache locking strategy, which may result in better performance at the cost of cache consistency. Setting this flag to false is only recommended for apps which create a new per request.
member.remarks: This flag is true by default. The default behavior is recommended.

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IAppConfig.ClientCapabilities
member.summary: Microsoft Identity specific OIDC extension that allows resource challenges to be resolved without interaction. Allows configuration of one or more client capabilities, e.g. "llt"
member.remarks: MSAL will transform these into a "access_token" claims request. See https://openid.net/specs/openid-connect-core-1_0-final.html#ClaimsParameter for details on claim requests. For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-claims-request

member.name: P:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IAppConfig.LegacyCacheCompatibilityEnabled
member.summary: Enables legacy ADAL cache serialization and deserialization.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalHttpClientFactory
member.summary: Factory responsible for creating HttpClient. See https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.net.http.httpclient?view=net-7.0#instancing for more details.
member.remarks: Implementations must be thread safe. Do not create a new HttpClient for each call to - this leads to socket exhaustion. If your app uses Integrated Windows Authentication, ensure is set to true.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalHttpClientFactory.GetHttpClient
member.summary: Method returning an HTTP client that will be used to communicate with Azure AD. This enables advanced scenarios. See https://aka.ms/msal-net-application-configuration.
member.returns: An HTTP client.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalMtlsHttpClientFactory
member.summary: A factory responsible for creating HttpClient instances configured for mutual TLS (mTLS). This factory is intended for use to secure communication with Azure AD using mTLS. For more details on HttpClient instancing, see https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/dotnet/api/system.net.http.httpclient?view=net-8.0#instancing.
member.remarks: Implementations of this interface must be thread-safe. It is important to reuse HttpClient instances to avoid socket exhaustion. Do not create a new HttpClient for each call to . If your application requires Integrated Windows Authentication, set to true. This interface is designed to support mTLS scenarios.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalMtlsHttpClientFactory.GetHttpClient(System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2)
member.summary: Returns an HttpClient configured with a certificate for mutual TLS authentication. This method enables advanced MTLS scenarios within Azure AD communications in MSAL.
member.param: The certificate to be used for MTLS authentication.
member.returns: An HttpClient instance configured with the specified certificate.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalSFHttpClientFactory
member.summary: Factory responsible for creating HttpClient with a custom server certificate validation callback. This is useful for the Service Fabric scenario where the server certificate validation is required using the server cert. See https://learn.microsoft.com/dotnet/api/system.net.http.httpclient?view=net-7.0#instancing for more details.
member.remarks: Implementations must be thread safe. Do not create a new HttpClient for each call to - this leads to socket exhaustion. If your app uses Integrated Windows Authentication, ensure is set to true.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.IMsalSFHttpClientFactory.GetHttpClient(System.Func{System.Net.Http.HttpRequestMessage,System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Certificate2,System.Security.Cryptography.X509Certificates.X509Chain,System.Net.Security.SslPolicyErrors,System.Boolean})
member.summary: Method returning an HTTP client that will be used to validate the server certificate through the provided callback. This method is useful when custom certificate validation logic is required, for the managed identity flow running on a service fabric cluster.
member.param: Callback to validate the server certificate for the Service Fabric.
member.returns: An HTTP client configured with the provided server certificate validation callback.

member.name: T:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ManagedIdentityApplicationBuilder
member.summary: Builder for managed identity applications.

member.name: M:Microsoft.Identity.Client.ManagedIdentityApplicationBuilder.Create(Microsoft.Identity.Client.AppConfig.ManagedIdentityId)
member.summary: Creates a ManagedIdentityApplicationBuilder from a user assigned managed identity clientID / resourceId / objectId. For example, for a system assigned managed identity use ManagedIdentityApplicationBuilder.Create(ManagedIdentityId.SystemAssigned) and for a user assigned managed identity use ManagedIdentityApplicationBuilder.Create(ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedClientId(clientId)) or ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedResourceId("resourceId") or ManagedIdentityId.WithUserAssignedObjectId("objectid"). For more details see https://aka.ms/msal-net-managed-identity
member.param: Configuration of the Managed Identity assigned to the resource.
member.returns: A from which to set more parameters, and to create a managed identity application instance

member.summary: Sets Extra Query Parameters for the query string in the HTTP authentication request.
member.param: This parameter will be appended as is to the query string in the HTTP authentication request to the authority as a string of segments of the form key=value separated by an ampersand character. The parameter can be null.
